title: Acid-base disturbances & anion gap
body_html:
Acid-base status
Acid-base status is typically assessed using arterial blood gases. normal values are shown below:

Table 1: Normal arterial blood gas values
TestNormal range
pH7.35-7.45
pO211-14 kPa
pCO24.5-6 kPa
Bicarbonate [HCO3-]24-30 mmol/l
Base excess-2 to +2 mmol/l
Lactate<2 mmol/L

Table 2: Signs and symptoms of acidosis and alkalosis
SystemAcidosisAlkalosis
Cardiovascularhypotension
peripheral vasodilatation
hyperkalaemia (arrhythmia/ECG changes)		tachycardia
hypokalaemia (arrhythmia/ECG changes)
Respiratorymetabolic acidosis
  • hypoventilation
respiratory acidosis
  • Kussmaul's
metabolic alkalosis
  • hyperventilation
respiratory alkalosis
  • hypoventilation
Neurologicallethargy
drowsiness
confusion
headache
coma		lethargy
drowsiness
confusion
tetany
temor
seizures
hyper-relexia
peripheral tingling/numbness
GastrointestinalNausea
Vomiting
Abdominal pain		Nausea
Vomiting
Abdominal pain

Disturbances
Acid-base disturbances may be classed as respiratory or metabolic in origin:
  • Respiratory origin where the disturbance is primarily of CO2 exchange
  • Metabolic origin where the disturbance is due to bicarbonate

A number of compensatory mechanisms exist to minimise pH disturbances. Metabolic buffering primarily involving bicarbonate, alteration in ventilation to alter pCO2 and renal excretion of acid. The relationship between acid (H+ ions), bicarbonate (HCO3- ) and carbon dioxide (CO2) can be shown by the equation below:

H+ + HCO3- ‡Œ H2CO3 ‡Œ CO2 + H2O

Respiratory acidosis
Respiratory acidosis refers to acidosis (pH less than 7.35) due to a respiratory cause. In practice this means the lungs are failing to remove carbon dioxide (CO2). The reason a rise in CO2 results in acidosis is because it combines with water then dissociates into bicarbonate and hydrogen ions. The increase in hydrogen ions is what causes acidosis.

Causes:
  • COPD
  • decompensation in other respiratory conditions e.g. life-threatening asthma / pulmonary oedema
  • sedative drugs causing hypoventilation e.g. benzodiazepine and opiate overdose

Respiratory Alkalosis
Respiratory alkalosis occurs when the CO2 level drops. This is most often due to increased ventilation rate and 'blowing off' CO2.
Causes:
  • hyperventilation
  • pulmonary embolism
  • salicylate poisoning*
  • CNS disorders: stroke, subarachnoid haemorrhage, encephalitis
  • altitude
  • pregnancy

*salicylate overdose leads to a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis. Early stimulation of the respiratory centre leads to a respiratory alkalosis whilst later the direct acid effects of salicylates (combined with acute renal failure) may lead to an acidosis

Metabolic acidosis
In metabolic acidosis, there is either excessive production of acid (H+) or loss of bicarbonate which leads to an overall increase in H+ and lowering of the pH.

In metabolic acidosis there is a low bicarbonate level as bicarbonate is consumed buffering the increased acid. There are a wide range of potential causes.


In metabolic acidosis breathing first becomes rapid and shallow. As acidosis worsens the breathing pattern gradually becomes deep, labored and gasping which is known as Kussmaul breathing


Causes:
Commonly causes classified according to the anion gap. The anion gap is the difference between the major measured cations and anions in plasma. It can be calculated using the equation below:

(Na+ + K+) - (Cl- + HCO3-)

A normal anion gap is in the range 9-14 mmol/l.

Low albumin may cause a low anion gap. Some labs may provide a corrected ion gap in patients with hypoalbuminaemia.


Normal anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis causes remembered using mnemonic HARDUPS:
  • H: H+ secretion failure from the kidneys i.e. renal tubular acidosis types 1 &4
  • A: Acetazolamide use (diamoxµ)
  • R: Renal tubular acidosis (type 2) with loss of HCO3- from the kidneys
  • D: Diarrhoea, loss of lower GI secretions including HCO3-
  • U: Ureteropelvic fistula with loss of HCO3- in secretions
  • P: Post-hypocapnia
  • S: Spironolactone

Raised anion gap metabolic acidosis causes remembered using mnemonic MUDPILERS:
  • M: Methanol
  • U: Uremia (chronic kidney failure)
  • D: Diabetic ketoacidosis
  • P: Paracetamol, Propylene glycol
  • I: Infection, Iron, Isoniazid (which can cause lactic acidosis in overdose), Inborn errors of metabolism
  • L: Lactic acidosis due to shock, hypoxia or drugs such as metformin
  • E: Ethylene glycol, ethanol*
  • R: Rhabdomyolysis
  • S: Salicylates (aspirin)
*Ethanol causes lactic acidosis and can produce ketones.

Metabolic alkalosis
May be caused by a loss of hydrogen ions or a gain of bicarbonate. It is due mainly to problems of the kidney or the gastrointestinal tract.

Causes
  • vomiting / aspiration (e.g. peptic ulcer leading to pyloric stenosis, nasogastric suction)
  • diuretics
  • liquorice, carbenoxolone
  • hypokalaemia
  • primary hyperaldosteronism
  • Cushing's syndrome
  • Bartter's syndrome
  • congenital adrenal hyperplasia

Mechanism of metabolic alkalosis
  • activation of renin-angiotensin II-aldosterone (RAA) system is a key factor
  • aldosterone causes reabsorption of Na+ in exchange for H+ in the distal convoluted tubule
  • ECF depletion (vomiting, diuretics) → Na+ and Cl- loss → activation of RAA system → raised aldosterone levels
  • in hypokalaemia, K+ shift from cells → ECF, alkalosis is caused by shift of H+ into cells to maintain neutrality

Glossary of terms used in College examinations
